intro: The pivotal question between Cursor and GitHub Copilot is how much of your setup stays portable: Cursor carries low lock-in and GitHub Copilot carries medium lock-in. Cursor is AI-first code editor forked from VS Code with deep repo context and agent mode. GitHub Copilot is AI code completion and chat embedded in GitHub, VS Code, and JetBrains. Pick Cursor when the job is vs code user wanting ai-first editor and you accept low lock-in; pick GitHub Copilot when it is apps and you accept medium lock-in. The data we have shows Cursor at mostly transparent pricing and docs with a polished developer experience, and GitHub Copilot at fully public pricing and documentation with a polished developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — Cursor is a poor fit for happy with github copilot in existing editor, and GitHub Copilot is a poor fit for simple projects. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the feature list.
quick_take: Cursor is for AI-first code editor forked from VS Code; GitHub Copilot is for AI code completion and chat embedded in; decide based on how portable the setup must stay.
verdict_a: Choose Cursor if a subscription at $20 and you want an entry-level free tier fits the budget and the work maps to AI-first code editor forked from VS Code with deep repo context and agent mode.
verdict_b: Choose GitHub Copilot if some ecosystem coupling is acceptable and the work lines up with AI code completion and chat embedded in GitHub, VS Code, and JetBrains.

faq.q: Is Cursor cheaper than GitHub Copilot?
faq.a: We can't verify it cleanly: Cursor starts at $20 (subscription), while GitHub Copilot (seat) doesn't publish an entry price. Request a quote from GitHub Copilot before assuming either side is cheaper.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Cursor to GitHub Copilot?
faq.a: Migration is possible in either direction. Cursor carries low lock-in and GitHub Copilot carries medium lock-in, so plan for meaningful but not blocking rework. Run both in parallel before you fully cut over.

Both Cursor and GitHub Copilot rate the same on developer experience (5/5). The decision on DX then comes down to taste — which CLI, UI, or workflow matches your team's habits. A short side-by-side trial is the quickest way to tell.

faq.q: Is GitHub Copilot a good alternative to Cursor?
faq.a: Yes — GitHub Copilot is a reasonable alternative to Cursor for vs code user wanting ai-first editor. The practical differences are subscription-vs-seat billing and low-vs-medium lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat GitHub Copilot as a credible swap.
